DATA VISUALIZATION AND INSIGHTS EXTRACTION¶

  • Univariate Analysis(Categorical or Continuous)
  • Bivariate Analysis (Categorical or Continuous)
  • Multivariate Analysis (Continuous)
  • Advanced Plots and Charts

1. LOAD THE REQUIRED LIBRARIES¶

In [1]:
#!pip install mpl_toolkits
In [2]:
import pandas as pd                       ## For data importation and dataframes manipulation
import numpy as np                        ## For Numeric Manipulation
import seaborn as sns                     ## For data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt           ## For data visualization
#from mpl_toolkits.mplt3d import Axes3D   ## For 3D Visualization

### Import warnings
import warnings                           ### For ignoring the warning messages
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

1.1. SET UP THE VISUALIZATION STYLE¶

In [3]:
sns.set_style("whitegrid")
plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = [10, 6]
plt.rcParams["font.size"] = 10

1.2. DATA IMPORTATION¶

In [4]:
df = pd.read_csv("GSSsubset.csv")

2. UNIVARIATE DATA VISUALIZATION¶

2.1. Histogram For the Distribution of Income¶

In [6]:
plt.figure(figsize = (14, 8))
sns.histplot(df['income'], bins = 20, kde = True,
            color = "skyblue", edgecolor = "black")
plt.title("Histogram Showing the Distribution of Income", fontsize = 14, fontweight = "bold")
plt.xlabel("Income ($)")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")

plt.axvline(df['income'].median(), color = 'red',
            linestyle = '--',
            label = f'Median: ${df['income'].median():,.0f}')

plt.axvline(df['income'].mean(), color = 'black',
            linestyle = '--',
            label = f'Mean: ${df['income'].mean():,.0f}')
plt.show()

Comparing the mean and median is a quick way to identify skewness (asymmetry) in a dataset. Because the mean is sensitive to extreme outliers, it shifts toward the "tail" of the data, while the median represents the middle point.

  1. Zero Skew (Symmetrical)

Rule: Mean = Median.

Shape: The distribution looks like a classic, symmetrical bell curve when graphed on a Scribbr Skewness Guide.

  1. Right/Positive Skew

Rule: Mean > Median

Shape: The data features a long tail extending toward the higher, positive numbers. The extreme high values pull the mean up, while the median stays closer to the bulk of the data.

  1. Left/Negative Skew

Rule: Mean < Median Shape: The data features a long tail extending toward the lower, negative numbers. The extreme low values pull the mean down, leaving the median higher than the mean.

In [7]:
df.drop(columns = ['id']).describe()
Out[7]:
income age height weight hrswrk
count 994.000000 994.000000 994.000000 994.000000 994.000000

2.2. Distribution of Income Using Box Plot¶

In [8]:
plt.figure(figsize = (10, 6))   ### Initialize the figure size
sns.boxplot(y = df['income'])    ### Pick the type of chart
plt.title("The Five Summary Statistics for Income", 
          fontsize = 14,
          fontweight = 'bold')
plt.ylabel("Income ($)")

plt.axhline(df['income'].mean(), color = "red",
            linestyle = '--', alpha = 0.9, label = 'Mean')

plt.axhline(df['income'].median(), color = "blue",
            linestyle = '--', alpha = 0.9, label = 'Median')
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
          
plt.show()                      ### Display the chart

2.3. Violin and Histogram for the Distribution of Ages¶

In [9]:
df.head()
Out[9]:
id sex degree income marital age height weight hrswrk
0 1 MALE BACHELOR 60967.50 DIVORCED 53 72 190 60
1 2 FEMALE BACHELOR 60967.50 MARRIED 26 60 97 40
2 4 FEMALE BACHELOR 10161.25 MARRIED 56 68 160 20
3 14 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 MARRIED 40 65 156 37
4 16 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 MARRIED 56 66 210 6
In [10]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize = (14, 6))

sns.histplot(df['age'], bins = 20, kde = True, ax = axes[0],
             color = 'steelblue', edgecolor = 'black')
axes[0].set_title("Age Distribution using Histogram", 
                  fontsize = 12, 
                  fontweight = 'bold')
axes[0].axvline(df['age'].mean(), color = 'red',
            linestyle = '--',
            label = f'Mean: ${df['age'].mean():,.0f}')
axes[0].axvline(df['age'].median(), color = 'blue',
            linestyle = '--',
            label = f'Median: ${df['age'].median():,.0f}')


sns.violinplot(y = df['age'], ax = axes[1], color = 'lightgreen')
axes[1].set_title("Age Distribution using Violin Plot", 
                  fontsize = 12, 
                  fontweight = 'bold')
axes[1].axhline(df['age'].mean(), color = 'red',
            linestyle = '--',
            label = f'Mean: ${df['age'].mean():,.0f}')
axes[1].axhline(df['age'].median(), color = 'blue',
            linestyle = '--',
            label = f'Median: ${df['age'].median():,.0f}')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

2.4. Scatter Plot Showing the Relationship Between Age and Income¶

In [11]:
plt.figure(figsize = (10, 6))
sns.regplot(data = df, x = 'age', y = 'income', scatter_kws = {'alpha': 0.9},
            line_kws = {'color': 'red', 'linewidth': 3})
plt.title("A Scetter Plot of Income Against Age", 
          fontsize = 14,
          fontweight = "bold")
plt.xlabel("Age (years)")
plt.ylabel("Income ($)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

2.4. Scatter Plot Showing the Relationship Between Age and Income¶

In [12]:
plt.figure(figsize = (10, 6))
sns.regplot(data = df, x = 'height', y = 'weight', scatter_kws = {'alpha': 0.9},
            line_kws = {'color': 'red', 'linewidth': 3})
plt.title("A Scetter Plot of Weight Against Height", 
          fontsize = 14,
          fontweight = "bold")
plt.xlabel("Height")
plt.ylabel("Weight")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

2.5. Joint Distribution PLot of Weight Against Height¶

In [13]:
joint_plot = sns.jointplot(data = df, x = "height", y = "weight",
                           kind = 'scatter', alpha = 0.8, color = 'steelblue',
                           marginal_kws = dict(bins = 50, fill = True)
                          )
joint_plot.fig.suptitle('A Joint Plot of Weight and Height Distribution',
                        fontsize = 14, fontweight = 'bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [14]:
print('='*110)
print(f"Correlation Between Weight and Height")
print('='*110)
corr_val = df['height'].corr(df['weight'])
print(f"Correlation: {corr_val:.3f}")

==============================================================================================================
Correlation Between Weight and Height
==============================================================================================================
Correlation: 0.482

3. Categorical Variables Analysis¶

3.1. Bar Showing the Distribution of Gender¶

In [15]:
df['sex'].value_counts()
Out[15]:
sex
MALE      507
FEMALE    487
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [16]:
plt.figure(figsize = (10,6))
ax = sns.countplot(data = df, x = "sex", palette = 'viridis',
                   edgecolor = 'black', linewidth = 1.5)

plt.title("Distribution of Gender in the General Social Survey",
          fontsize = 14, 
          fontweight = 'bold')

plt.xlabel("Gender")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")

total = len(df)
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = f'{100*p.get_height()/total:.1f}%'
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width()/2
    y = p.get_height()+10
    ax. annotate (percentage, (x, y), 
                  ha = 'center', 
                  fontweight = 'bold')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize = (10,6))
ax = sns.countplot(data = df, x = "degree", palette = 'Set1',
                   edgecolor = 'black', linewidth = 1.5)

plt.title("Distribution of Degree Qualification in the General Social Survey",
          fontsize = 14, 
          fontweight = 'bold')

plt.xlabel("Degree")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")

total = len(df)
for p in ax.patches:

    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width()/2
    y = p.get_height()+10
    ax. annotate (percentage, (x, y), 
                  ha = 'center', 
                  fontweight = 'bold')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

ax = sns.countplot(data = df, x = "marital", palette = 'Set1',
                   edgecolor = 'black', linewidth = 1.5)

plt.title("Distribution of Marital Status in the General Social Survey",

plt.tight_layout()

3.3. Horizontal Bar Chart Showing the Distribution of Degree Qualification¶

In [36]:
plt.figure(figsize = (10,6))
degree_counts = df['degree'].value_counts()
ax = sns.barplot(x = degree_counts.values, y = degree_counts.index,
                 palette = 'Set2', edgecolor = 'red')
plt.title("Bar Chart Showing the Frequency of Educational Qualification", 
          fontsize = 14, fontweight = 'bold')
plt.xlabel("Frequency")
plt.ylabel("Degree Qualification")

for i, v in enumerate(degree_counts.values):
    ax.text(v+10, i, str(v), va = 'center',
            fontweight = 'bold')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show

print(degree_counts)

degree
HIGH SCHOOL       491
BACHELOR          209
GRADUATE          125
JUNIOR COLLEGE     92
LT HIGH SCHOOL     77
Name: count, dtype: int64

3.4. Pie Chart Showing the Distribution of Degree Qualification¶

In [53]:
plt.figure(figsize = (14, 6))
degree_counts = df['degree'].value_counts()

color = plt.cm.Set3(np.linspace(0,1,len(degree_counts)))
#color

plt.pie(
    degree_counts,
    labels = degree_counts.index,
    autopct = '%1.3f%%',
    colors = color,
    startangle = 90,
    explode = [0.01]*len(degree_counts)
)

plt.title("Pie Chart Showing the Distribution of Degree Qualification",
          fontsize = 14, 
          fontweight = 'bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

3.5. Pie Chart and Bar Chart for Degree Qualification¶

In [60]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1,2, figsize = (14, 6))
degree_counts = df['degree'].value_counts()
color = plt.cm.Set3(np.linspace(0,1,len(degree_counts)))

axes[0].pie(degree_counts, labels = degree_counts.index,
            autopct = '%1.3f%%',
            colors = color,
            startangle = 90,
            explode = [0.01]*len(degree_counts))
axes[0].set_title("Pie Chart Showing the Distribution of Degree Qualification")

sns.barplot(x = degree_counts.values, y = degree_counts.index, ax = axes[1],
                 palette = 'Set2', edgecolor = 'red')
axes[1].set_title("Bar Chart Showing Degree Qualification",
                  fontweight = 'bold',
                  fontsize = 12)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Frequency")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Degree Qualification")
    
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

2 by 2 Plots- Four Chart is one Plot¶

In [83]:
# Style
sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Degree frequencies
degree_counts = df['degree'].value_counts()
colors = plt.cm.Set3(np.linspace(0, 1, len(degree_counts)))

# Create 2x2 layout
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(15, 12))

# -----------------------------
# 1. Pie Chart
# -----------------------------
axes[0, 0].pie(
    degree_counts,
    labels=degree_counts.index,
    autopct='%1.1f%%',
    startangle=90,
    colors=colors,
    explode=[0.03] * len(degree_counts)
)
axes[0, 0].set_title(
    "Distribution of Degree Qualification",
    fontsize=13,
    fontweight='bold'
)

# -----------------------------
# 2. Horizontal Bar Chart
# -----------------------------
sns.barplot(
    x=degree_counts.values,
    y=degree_counts.index,
    palette='Set2',
    edgecolor='black',
    ax=axes[0, 1]
)

axes[0, 1].set_title(
    "Frequency of Degree Qualification",
    fontsize=13,
    fontweight='bold'
)
axes[0, 1].set_xlabel("Frequency")
axes[0, 1].set_ylabel("Degree")

# Add frequency labels
for i, v in enumerate(degree_counts.values):
    axes[0, 1].text(v + 2, i, str(v), va='center', fontsize=10)

# -----------------------------
# 3. Histogram of Income
# -----------------------------
sns.histplot(
    data=df,
    x='income',
    bins=20,
    kde=True,
    color='steelblue',
    edgecolor='black',
    ax=axes[1, 0]
)

axes[1, 0].set_title(
    "Distribution of Income",
    fontsize=13,
    fontweight='bold'
)
axes[1, 0].set_xlabel("Income")
axes[1, 0].set_ylabel("Frequency")

# -----------------------------
# 4. Boxplot: Income by Degree
# -----------------------------
sns.boxplot(
    data=df,
    x='degree',
    y='income',
    palette='Pastel1',
    ax=axes[1, 1]
)

axes[1, 1].set_title(
    "Income by Degree Qualification",
    fontsize=13,
    fontweight='bold'
)
axes[1, 1].set_xlabel("Degree")
axes[1, 1].set_ylabel("Income")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Correlational Heatmap¶

In [85]:
# Select continuous variables
continuous_vars = ['age', 'income', 'hrswrk', 'height', 'weight']

# Compute correlation matrix
corr_matrix = df[continuous_vars].corr(method='pearson')

# Plot heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

sns.heatmap(
    corr_matrix,
    annot=True,          # Display correlation coefficients
    cmap='coolwarm',     # Color palette
    fmt='.2f',           # Two decimal places
    linewidths=0.5,
    square=True,
    cbar_kws={'label': 'Pearson Correlation'}
)

plt.title(
    'Correlation Heatmap of Continuous Variables',
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight='bold'
)

plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.yticks(rotation=0)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Pairplot¶

In [86]:
# Continuous variables
continuous_vars = ['age', 'income', 'hrswrk', 'height', 'weight']

# Pairplot
pair = sns.pairplot(
    df[continuous_vars],
    diag_kind='hist',      # Histogram on the diagonal
    corner=True,           # Plot only the lower triangle
    plot_kws={
        'alpha': 0.7,
        's': 40,
        'edgecolor': 'black'
    },
    diag_kws={
        'bins': 20,
        'edgecolor': 'black'
    }
)

pair.fig.suptitle(
    'Pairwise Relationships Among Continuous Variables',
    fontsize=16,
    fontweight='bold',
    y=1.02
)

plt.show()

3.6. Income by Degree Qualification¶

In [82]:
sns.set_theme(style = 'whitegrid')
plt.figure(figsize = (10, 6))

sns.barplot(data = df,
           x = 'degree',
           y = 'income',
           estimator = 'mean',
           errorbar='sd',
           palette = 'Set1')
plt.title("Average Income by Degree Qualification", 
          fontsize = 14, 
          fontweight = 'bold')
plt.xlabel("Degree")
plt.ylabel("Mean Income")
plt.xticks(rotation = 0)
plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

3.7. Comparative Bar Chart of Income by Degree Qualification Across Gender¶

In [75]:
sns.set_theme(style = 'whitegrid')
plt.figure(figsize = (10, 6))

sns.barplot(data = df,
            x = 'degree',
            y = 'income',
            hue = 'sex',
            estimator = 'mean',
            errorbar = 'sd',
            palette = 'Set1')

plt.title("Average Income by Degree Qualification Across Gender", 
          fontsize = 14, 
          fontweight = 'bold')
plt.xlabel("Degree")
plt.ylabel("Mean Income")

plt.legend(title = "Participant's Gender")
plt.xticks(rotation = 0)
plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()
In [76]:
plt.figure(figsize=(11,7))

ax = sns.barplot(
    data=df,
    x="degree",
    y="income",
    hue="sex",
    estimator="mean",
    errorbar=("ci", 95),
    palette="viridis"
)
# Improve appearance
ax.set_title(
    "Mean Income by Educational Degree and Sex",
    fontsize=18,
    fontweight="bold"
)

ax.set_xlabel("Educational Degree", fontsize=14)
ax.set_ylabel("Mean Income", fontsize=14)

plt.xticks(fontsize=11)
plt.yticks(fontsize=11)

plt.legend(
    title="Sex",
    title_fontsize=12,
    fontsize=11
)

sns.despine
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
